title: How to rebuild trust in your own body after it was violated
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I know what it's like to feel like a stranger in your own skin. To look at your hands and wonder whose they are. To feel a wave of panic when your heart beats...
metaDescription: I know what it's like to feel like a stranger in your own skin. To look at your hands and wonder whose they are. To feel a wave of panic when your heart b...
htmlContent:
I know what it's like to feel like a stranger in your own skin. To look at your hands and wonder whose they are. To feel a wave of panic when your heart beats faster, even though you're just walking to the kitchen. The body that was supposed to be home became a crime scene. And now you're supposed to just trust it again? How?

Here's the thing. Trust isn't a switch you flip. It's a slow, awkward rebuilding. Like learning to stand after your legs were broken. You don't start by running. You start by noticing that the floor is still there. You start by breathing, even when your chest says no. You start by letting your body know, one tiny moment at a time, that you're not going to abandon it again.

I'm going to be honest with you. This work is hard. It's not the kind of hard where you grit your teeth and push through. It's the kind of hard where you sit with the shaking and the nausea and the rage, and you don't run. But I promise you, there is a way back. Not to the body you had before. But to a body you can live in again.

Why your body feels like the enemy

Let's start with the science. Because understanding what happened can take some of the shame out of it. Your nervous system is designed to keep you alive. When you experience violation, your brain and body do something . They shift into survival mode. Your amygdala, the smoke detector, goes into overdrive. Your prefrontal cortex, the rational part of your brain, starts to take a back seat. Your body stays on high alert, scanning for threats, even when the danger is long gone.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades studying this. He showed that trauma isn't just a memory stored in your mind. It's a physical imprint. Your muscles hold tension. Your gut clenches. Your breath becomes shallow. Your body remembers what happened, even when your mind wants to forget. And that's the betrayal. Your own body becomes the carrier of the memory. It feels like it's working against you.

But here's the thing. Your body isn't the enemy. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's trying to protect you. The problem is, the alarm system got stuck. It doesn't know that the threat is over. So you have to teach it, gently, that you're safe now. That's the rebuilding. Not fighting your body. Befriending it.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work gives a framework for understanding how complex trauma shapes your nervous system and your sense of self. It's not a cure. But it's a map. And sometimes a map is all you need to start walking.

Starting where you are: noticing

I'm not going to tell you to do a full body scan right now. If you're like me, that advice makes you want to throw something. Because the moment you pay attention to your body, you feel the tightness, the nausea, the urge to crawl out of your skin. So let's start smaller. Much smaller.

Here's an exercise that feels almost too simple. But stay with me here. Pick one spot in your body that feels neutral. Not painful. Not numb. Just there. Maybe it's your left earlobe. Maybe it's the arch of your right foot. Maybe it's the space between your eyebrows. Just one spot. And notice what it feels like. Not to change it. Not to fix it. Just to notice.

That's it. That's the beginning. You're not trying to feel your whole body. You're not trying to relax. You're just making contact. Like reaching out a hand to a scared animal. You don't grab it. You just let it know you're there.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, calls this pendulation. You move between what feels safe and what feels uncomfortable, slowly expanding your window of tolerance. Your nervous system can't handle too much at once. It needs to know that it can come back to safety. So you touch the edge of the pain, and then you pull back. Touch and pull back. Over and over. That's how trust is built. Not in grand gestures. In tiny, repeated returns.

Think about it. If someone betrayed you, you wouldn't trust them again just because they said sorry once. You'd watch them. You'd test them. You'd see if they showed up consistently. That's what you're doing with your body. You're showing up, day after day, even when it's hard. Even when you don't feel like it. Especially then.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete exercises to track your nervous system states and build skills for self-regulation. Sometimes having a structure helps when your own mind feels too chaotic to guide you.

Learning to listen without fixing

Most of us were taught that our bodies are problems to be solved. You feel anxious? Calm down. You feel sad? Cheer up. You feel pain? Fix it. But your body isn't a broken machine. It's a living system trying to communicate with you. And the first step to rebuilding trust is learning to listen without immediately trying to change what you hear.

This is where things get uncomfortable. Because listening means sitting with sensations that feel unbearable. It means feeling the knot in your stomach without trying to untie it. It means letting the tears come without wiping them away. It means saying, "I see you. I hear you. I'm not going anywhere." To yourself. To your own body.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who integrates Internal Family Systems and Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, talks about this as "befriending" the parts of you that hold the trauma. You don't have to like them. You don't have to agree with them. But you have to be willing to be with them. Because the parts of you that feel like enemies are actually protectors. They're doing their job. They just need to know that you're in charge now. That you can handle it.

Here's a practice that helped me. When I notice a wave of panic or dread, I put my hand on my chest. Not to stop the feeling. Just to say, "I'm here." I don't try to breathe deeply. I don't try to think positive thoughts. I just let my hand rest there, warm and present. And I wait. Sometimes the feeling passes. Sometimes it stays. But something shifts. The isolation breaks. I'm not alone in it anymore.

Right? That's the core of it. You're not trying to make the feeling go away. You're trying to show your body that it's not alone. That you're willing to stay with it. That's how trust is rebuilt. Not through force. Through presence.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name the reality of complex trauma and the long road to reclaiming your body and your life. It's a sobering read. But it's also validating. Knowing that what you're experiencing has a name and a path forward can be deeply grounding.

Moving from survival to connection

Here's where it gets tricky. Because the nervous system's job is to keep you alive. And for a long time, the way it kept you alive was by cutting you off from your body. Numbing out. Dissociating. Shutting down. That was the strategy that worked. And your nervous system doesn't want to give up a strategy that saved your life. Even if it's no longer needed.

So when you start trying to reconnect with your body, you might feel resistance. You might feel more pain. You might feel like you're getting worse before you get better. That's normal. That's the system recalibrating. It's like a computer that's been running on emergency power for years. When you plug it back into the main grid, things can get weird. Lights flicker. Programs crash. But eventually, it stabilizes.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about the social engagement system. It's the part of your nervous system that allows you to feel safe in connection with others. But when you've been violated, that system gets hijacked. Your body goes into fight, flight, or freeze. It can't tell the difference between a threat and a friendly face. So part of rebuilding trust is teaching your body that connection can be safe again.

This doesn't mean you have to jump into relationships. It means you start with yourself. You practice making eye contact with your own reflection. You practice softening your voice when you talk to yourself. You practice letting your shoulders drop, just a little, when you're alone in your room. You practice the micro-movements of safety. And over time, your nervous system starts to learn. "Oh. This is what safe feels like. I can let my guard down. Just a little."

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She translates Polyvagal Theory into simple, daily practices that help you map your nervous system states and find your way back to regulation. It's not about fixing. It's about noticing and gently shifting.

The slow, awkward return

I'm not going to tell you that you'll wake up one day and suddenly trust your body completely. That's not how it works. Trust is built in the small moments. In the morning when you stretch instead of clenching. In the shower when you let the water run over your shoulders without flinching. In the night when you feel a flutter of fear and you put your hand on your heart instead of reaching for the bottle or the screen or the escape.

It's awkward. It's clumsy. You'll fall. You'll forget. You'll go back to old patterns. And that's okay. Because every time you come back, you're rebuilding the trust. Every time you notice, you're saying, "I'm still here." Every time you choose presence over numbness, you're telling your body, "You matter. You're worth staying with."

Wild, right? That the thing that was taken from you, the sense of safety in your own skin, can be rebuilt. Not by forgetting what happened. But by remembering that you survived. And that your body, the one that carried you through, deserves your gratitude. Not your war.

You don't have to love your body. You don't have to feel grateful every day. You just have to be willing to show up. Again and again. Until the stranger becomes a friend. Until the enemy becomes an ally. Until you can say, "This is my body. It's been through hell. And it's still here. We're still here."

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • Anchored (paid link)
